headline: Colorado's Statement Win Reshapes Early Season Pecking Order
subhead: A dominant road upset of Georgia and Texas's dismantling of Ohio State suggest the preseason favorites may not hold their thrones for long.
body: Colorado walked into Athens and didn't just beat Georgia—they beat them convincingly, 42 to 28. That's not a close loss that could've gone either way. That's a team that was supposed to be a national title contender getting outplayed from start to finish. The Buffaloes' presence in the power poll's top five was generous heading into the week. Now it looks prescient. What makes this result stick is that Colorado didn't sneak past Georgia; they imposed their style of play and Georgia had no answer. That's the kind of road win that either launches a season or exposes a team as fundamentally overrated. Colorado's coaching and roster just looked better when it mattered most.

Texas made a similar statement at Ohio State, winning 29 to 15 in a game that never felt close. The Longhorns ranked fourth in the power poll and proved it wasn't charity. But what's notable here is that Ohio State, a program that should never be dismissed, looked pedestrian offensively—just 15 points at home against a team that's still settling into its new conference. If there's a weakness in the Big Ten, Columbus might have just broadcast it to the rest of the country.

The other seismic shift came at Tennessee, which held off Penn State 26 to 20 to remain the power poll's top-ranked team. That's a real win, the kind that builds a resume. Washington's 35 to 28 victory over Miami looked cleaner on the surface, but Tennessee had to grind it out against a Big Ten team in a stadium that wanted them to lose. That's where seasons are actually built. Meanwhile, LSU squeaked past Oregon 27 to 24 in what feels increasingly like a rubber match between two teams that haven't solved their deepest problems yet.

Arkansas and Notre Dame produced the week's most lopsided result, the Irish winning 35 to 10, but that's almost beside the point. The meaningful story is that Colorado and Texas have crashed the party at the top, and neither looks like they're leaving. If the power poll is right and Tennessee stays composed, we're watching an actual open field form at the summit rather than a coronation of preseason favorites. Georgia's loss stings worst because it came at home, and Ohio State's defeat carries long-term implications for how we should value the Big Ten this fall.
